title: The pronouns y and en
description: A2 French for English speakers: how y replaces à + place/thing (Tu vas à Paris? J'y vais.) and en replaces de + thing or a partitive/quantity (Tu veux du café? J'en veux. J'en ai trois.). Both pronouns go before the verb. Explained in English.

cards.back: I'm going there. (y = to Paris / à Paris)
cards.notes: **y** replaces **à + place** (or any location phrase): *Tu vas à Paris ? J'y vais.* (Are you going to Paris? I'm going there.) Place it **before the conjugated verb**.

cards.back: I'm thinking about it. (y = à ce problème / à cette idée)
cards.notes: **y** also replaces **à + thing** (not a person) with verbs like *penser à, répondre à, jouer à*: *Tu penses à ce problème ? J'y pense.* English often drops the pronoun entirely ('I'm thinking') — French requires **y**.
cards.hint: y = à + thing/place. Never use y for à + person.

cards.notes: **il y a** is a frozen idiom you already know from A1 — literally 'it there has'. The **y** here is the same pronoun meaning 'there' (à cet endroit). *Il y a un chat dans le jardin.* (There is a cat in the garden.)

cards.back: I want some. (en = du café / de la soupe)
cards.notes: **en** replaces **de + thing**, including **partitive articles** (du, de la, des): *Tu veux du café ? J'en veux.* (Do you want some coffee? I want some.) English often says just 'I want some' — French always keeps **en**.

When a **quantity or number** is mentioned, **en** is still required even though English drops it: *Tu as des frères ? J'en ai trois.* (Do you have brothers? I have three.) English says 'I have three' — French says **j'en ai trois** (literally 'I have three of them').
cards.hint: English: 'I have three.' French: j'en ai trois — keep en!

cards.back: I need it / I need some. (en = de + thing, with avoir besoin de)
cards.notes: **avoir besoin de** uses **en** to replace *de + thing*: *Tu as besoin de ce livre ? J'en ai besoin.* (Do you need this book? I need it.) Compare: *avoir besoin de quelqu'un* → **lui/leur** (à + person), NOT en.

cards.back: y replaces à + place/thing; en replaces de + thing
cards.notes: The key contrast: **y** = **à** + place/thing. **en** = **de** + thing (or partitive). Neither is used for **people**: *à + person* → **lui/leur** (indirect object pronoun). Example: *Je lui parle.* (I'm talking to him/her.)

cards.front: J'y vais. / Je n'y vais pas. / J'en ai deux.
cards.back: y and en go before the conjugated verb (after ne in negation)
cards.notes: Like all object pronouns, **y** and **en** go **directly before the verb**: *J'**y** vais, Il **en** prend.* In negation, they stay between *ne* and the verb: *Je **n'y** vais pas, Il **n'en** prend pas.*

cards.front: Tu vas au marché ? Oui, j'y vais.
cards.back: Are you going to the market? Yes, I'm going there.
cards.notes: *y* stands for *au marché* (= à + le marché). The question uses the full noun phrase; the answer replaces it with **y** before the verb.

cards.front: Tu viens de Paris ? Oui, j'en viens.
cards.back: Are you coming from Paris? Yes, I'm coming from there.
cards.notes: **en** can also replace **de + place** with movement verbs like *venir de, revenir de, sortir de*: *j'en viens* = I'm coming from there. This is the exact mirror of **y** (à + place): **en** handles **de + place**.

cards.back: à + person stays as lui/leur — never y
cards.notes: **y** never replaces **à + person**. For people, use the indirect object pronouns **lui** (him/her) or **leur** (them): *Je parle à Marie → Je **lui** parle.* This is a common mistake for English speakers who might try to use **y** or drop the pronoun entirely.

steps.title: The pronoun y — replacing à + place or thing
steps.body:
# The pronoun y

**y** (pronounced like the letter 'e' in 'feet') replaces a phrase beginning with **à** when it refers to a **place or thing** — never a person.

## Uses of y

| What y replaces | Example with noun | Example with y |
|---|---|---|
| à + **place** | *Tu vas **à Paris** ?* | *J'**y** vais.* (I'm going there.) |
| à + **place** | *Il est **au bureau**.* | *Il **y** est.* (He is there.) |
| à + **thing** (*penser à*, *jouer à*, *répondre à*) | *Tu penses **à ce problème** ?* | *J'**y** pense.* (I'm thinking about it.) |

## Position

**y** goes **directly before the conjugated verb**:
- Affirmative: *J'**y** vais.*
- Negative: *Je **n'y** vais pas.*

## il y a

The A1 expression **il y a** (there is / there are) uses the same **y**: it literally means 'there has' — a frozen reminder of how y works.

## Watch out!

English often drops 'there' entirely ('I'm going' instead of 'I'm going there'). French **always** keeps **y**: *J'y vais* — never just *Je vais*.

steps.title: The pronoun en — replacing de + thing and partitives
steps.body:
## The pronoun en

**en** replaces a phrase beginning with **de** when it refers to a **thing** (not a person). This includes **partitive articles** (du, de la, de l', des) and **quantities**.

## Uses of en

| What en replaces | Example with noun | Example with en |
|---|---|---|
| **Partitive** (du / de la / des) | *Tu veux **du café** ?* | *J'**en** veux.* (I want some.) |
| **de + thing** after an expression | *Tu as besoin **de ce livre** ?* | *J'**en** ai besoin.* (I need it.) |
| **Quantity + noun** | *Tu as **des frères** ?* | *J'**en** ai trois.* (I have three.) |
| **de + place** (movement verbs) | *Il vient **de Lyon**.* | *Il **en** vient.* (He comes from there.) |

## The quantity trap — biggest English-speaker error

When a number or quantity is mentioned, **en is still mandatory**:

> English: *I have **three**.* (no pronoun)
> French: *J'**en** ai trois.* (literally: I have three of them)

Dropping **en** in French sounds as wrong as omitting a subject in English.

steps.title: y vs en vs lui/leur — the full picture
steps.body:
## y vs en vs lui/leur

The three pronoun families cover three preposition + noun patterns:

| Preposition | Refers to | Pronoun | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **à** + place/thing | a place or non-person thing | **y** | *J'y vais.* / *J'y pense.* |
| **de** + thing | a thing (incl. partitive) | **en** | *J'en veux.* / *J'en ai trois.* |
| **à** + **person** | a person | **lui / leur** | *Je lui parle.* |

## Why à + person never becomes y

Because **lui/leur** already handles indirect objects with people. *y* is reserved for places and things. This is a frequent error:

- **Wrong:** *Je y parle.* (trying to say 'I talk to him')
- **Right:** *Je **lui** parle.*

## Both pronouns go before the verb

*J'**y** pense, il **en** a deux, elle **n'en** prend pas, nous **n'y** allons plus.*

> Memory hook: **y = à (place/thing)**, **en = de (thing/quantity)**, **lui/leur = à (person)**.

steps.title: Match the pronoun to what it replaces

exercise.prompt: Match each pronoun to the phrase it replaces.
Translate: *Are you going to Paris? Yes, I'm going there.* (use y)
accept: Tu vas à Paris ? Oui, j'y vais.
Tu vas à Paris? Oui, j'y vais.
Tu vas à Paris ? Oui, j'y vais
tu vas à Paris ? oui, j'y vais.
Tu vas à Paris? J'y vais.
Tu vas à Paris ? J'y vais.
exercise.hint: Replace à Paris with y before the verb: j'y vais.
distractors: Tu vas à Paris ? Oui, je vais.
Tu vas à Paris ? Oui, j'en vais.
Tu vas à Paris ? Oui, je vais y.
Tu vas à Paris ? Oui, j'y vas.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *Do you want some coffee? Yes, I want some.* (use en)
accept: Tu veux du café ? Oui, j'en veux.
Tu veux du café? Oui, j'en veux.
Tu veux du café ? Oui, j'en veux
tu veux du café ? oui, j'en veux.
Tu veux du café ? J'en veux.
Tu veux du café? J'en veux.
exercise.hint: du café is a partitive → replace with en before the verb: j'en veux.
distractors: Tu veux du café ? Oui, je veux.
Tu veux du café ? Oui, j'y veux.
Tu veux du café ? Oui, je veux en.
Tu veux du café ? Oui, j'en veut.

steps.title: en with a quantity — the English-speaker trap

exercise.prompt: Translate: *Do you have brothers? I have three.* (French must keep en — don't drop it!)
accept: Tu as des frères ? J'en ai trois.
Tu as des frères? J'en ai trois.
Tu as des frères ? J'en ai trois
tu as des frères ? j'en ai trois.
Tu as des frères ? Oui, j'en ai trois.
Tu as des frères? Oui, j'en ai trois.
exercise.hint: English drops the pronoun ('I have three') — French keeps en: j'en ai trois.
distractors: Tu as des frères ? J'ai trois.
Tu as des frères ? J'y ai trois.
Tu as des frères ? J'en ai des trois.
Tu as des frères ? J'ai trois frères en.

exercise.prompt: Replace the underlined place with the correct pronoun. (Il va au marché → ?)

accept: y

exercise.hint: au marché = à + le marché → replace with y before the verb.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the correct pronoun. (They need it — avoir besoin de ce document)
exercise.sentence: Ils ___ ont besoin pour la réunion.

accept: en

exercise.hint: avoir besoin de + thing → replace de + thing with en.

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *I'm thinking about it.* (penser à quelque chose → use y)
exercise.hint: y replaces à + thing; it goes before the verb: j'y pense.

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *She doesn't want any.* (en in a negative sentence)
exercise.hint: In negation: ne … pas wraps the verb, but en stays between ne and the verb.

exercise.prompt: Match each underlined phrase to the pronoun that replaces it.
A woman says she is going to the library. Which sentence is correct?
exercise.hint: à + place → y, placed before the verb.
